results.title: Ocean Orchestrator - Run compute jobs from your editor
results.content:
Ocean Nodes are designed to manage failures locally, keeping compute job execution predictable and controlled. If a node goes down mid-run, the job can be restarted on the same node once it becomes available again. Funds are released from escrow only when the node explicitly marks a job as successful.Rerouting is handled by the user, in line with the Ocean Network ethos of giving users full control over which resources are used.This differs when a failure is caused by the algorithm itself. In that case, the job is treated as unsuccessful because the execution failed, not because the node was unavailable. See the dedicated FAQ for algorithm failures. [...] Results, logs, and metrics are saved automatically to your local folder the moment the job completes

## Stop losing time before the run

GPU compute workflows often slow you down before the job even starts. Comparing options, cloud setup, switching dashboards, and paying for idle infrastructure kills momentum.

You want a direct path: choose your GPUs, run a job, pay only for execution time. [...] ## Stop losing build time to cloud setup.

With traditional cloud, provisioning comes before coding. You're comparing options,
setting up environments, and paying for idle capacity before a single job runs.
Ocean Network removes that entirely. Pick a node, submit your job, pay for what ran.

### No setup before you run

Choose your environment in the dashboard and launch. No provisioning scripts, no SSH, no config files.

### Pay only for what ran

Escrow locks your budget up front. You're charged for actual runtime only, not idle capacity, not failed jobs.

### Outputs back locally, automatically

No retrieval step. Results and logs land in your local folder when the job completes.

results.title: Ocean Reviews (2026) | Product Hunt
results.content:
Based on 23 reviews

Review Ocean ?

Reviewers are overwhelmingly positive, with speed the clearest theme: many say Ocean answers almost instantly and feels noticeably faster than ChatGPT or other tools, while still giving clear, accurate, well-structured responses. Users also repeatedly praise its privacy and decentralized approach, describing it as free from tracking, censorship, and corporate control. File analysis, voice features, chat organization, and the clean interface get frequent mentions too. There is little criticism in the reviews, so feedback centers mostly on performance, privacy, and anticipation for upcoming features like Copilot and ORI.

+20

Summarized with AI

Pros

Cons

Reviews

Most Informative

Subscribe

Pros

fast performance(76)

censorship-resistant(35) [...] Okay, I have to say it - Ocean is ridiculously fast. I’ve been using ChatGPT, Copilot, and Claude for months, but nothing compares to this. I threw a LeetCode Medium problem at it, and it solved it 15.5x faster than OpenAI’s O1 mode. And no, I’m not exaggerating—I actually timed it. But here’s what really got me: Ocean isn’t just fast, it’s decentralized. That means no big corporations tracking my searches, no weird restrictions on what I can ask, and no “Sorry, I can’t answer that” nonsense. Just pure, uncensored AI that works exactly how I need it to. The PDF and spreadsheet analysis is a game-changer too. I uploaded a huge report, expecting it to take forever, but Ocean broke it down in seconds. That alone makes it worth using. And don’t even get me started on the UI—it’s so clean and [...] AI is moving fast, but Ocean just took things to a whole new level. This is the first AI search engine that actually makes sense. Here’s what makes Ocean special: No censorship - No one controlling what AI can or can’t say. No tracking - Unlike OpenAI, Ocean isn’t collecting your data behind the scenes. O Voice - A voice assistant that actually works without weird delays. Speed - 15x faster than OpenAI. File uploads + deep analysis That alone makes it a must try, but then there’s the speed. I asked it a question, and the answer came back before I even realized it had loaded. It’s that fast. I also LOVE that it analyzes files. I uploaded a big Excel sheet with messy data, and Ocean gave me an instant breakdown. No more wasting time manually digging through numbers. And the UI? Chef’s kiss.

Yann LeCun’s Post

Yes, nothing more frustrating than seeing an awesome piece of software you contributed to sitting on a digital shelf, gathering digital dust, ignored, because your employers doesn't believe in open source.
I suffered from this at AT&T.
One big reason I'm at Meta is that open sourcing infrastructure software and research code is in the DNA of the company.

Senior Software Engineer at Meta

I’m thrilled to share that my team just open-sourced Ocean, a framework for Computer Vision and Mixed Reality applications. It’s published under the MIT license. For details and demos check out the link below: [...] Meta Open Source (@metaopensource) on Threads

threads.net

Dear Professor Yann LeCun Thank you for your generous sharing of knowledge and code. What is your open source advise to a startup company which has solved many of the problems with today’s AI (and which is not LLM based) such as robustness and many step complex reasoning and planning?

Director of Engineering @ AMD AI

+1. 
I have used VisionTools while at Microsoft. It was a good library to work with, especially for image pyramids.

Interesting view

For those who care about what they do, and actually know what they are talking about, this phenomenum is probably endemic, well beyond code and tools.

MS in AI Graduate @ Northeastern University

My main takeaway from this is it’s time to check out Ocean

results.title: LLMs are fundamentally incapable of doing software engineering. : r/ChatGPTCoding
results.content:
Defining the specific boundary between what LLMs are good at vs bad at is a difficult and highly active area of research. That this line is fuzzy, that it's frustrating, just means we don't really know how to use them, not that they are "doomed to fail" or "incapable of software engineering."

> with each subsequent prompt/attempt, the AI has a decreasing chance of solving the problem [...] 3 more replies

3 more replies



But none of it feels like a hard wall more like yeah it's entirely possible that'll be a hard problem to solve but also entirely possible it could be solved like in a few months.
